nameMeanings.name: Abram
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, German, Dutch, Polish, Slovenian, Croatian, and Jewish; Hungarian (Ábrám): from a shortened form of the Biblical personal name Abraham .

English: habitational name from a place near Manchester, recorded as Adburgham in the 12th century, Abraham in the late 14th century, and Abram in the 15th. The placename is from the Old English female personal name Ēadburg + hām ‘homestead’, meaning ‘Ēadburg's homestead’.

variants.name: Abraham
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Mathai, Mohan, Raju, Saji, Prasad, Anil, Babu, Biju, Leela, Sajan, Shibu, Suma.

English, French, Spanish, German, Slovenian, Ethiopian, and Assyrian/Chaldean; Hungarian (Ábrahám), Slovak (mainly Abrahám, also Ábrahám), Czech (also Abrahám): from the Hebrew personal name ʾAbraham (modern spelling Avraham), borne by the Biblical patriarch revered by Jews as the founding father of the Jewish people (Genesis 11–25) and by Muslims as founder of all the Semitic peoples, both Hebrew and Arab (compare Ibrahim ). The original name of the Biblical patriarch was probably Abram , meaning ‘high father’ (from ab father, ram ‘exalted’), while its later form is explained in Genesis 17:5 as being derived from Hebrew ab hamon goyim ‘father of a multitude of nations’. It was widely used as a personal name among Christians as well as Jews in the Middle Ages. The name Abraham is also found among Christians in southern India, but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US. As a surname of Hungarian origin Abraham is also found in Romania. — Note: Since Ethiopians do not have hereditary surnames, the Ethiopian name was registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.
Irish: adoption of the English name (see 1 above) as an equivalent of Gaelic Mac an Bhreitheamhan ‘son of the judge’. See McBroom and compare Breheny .

variants.name: Abramov
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Russian Boris, Oleg, Mikhail, Vyacheslav, Leonid, Dmitriy, Grigoriy, Iosif, Vladimir, Yury, Aleksey, Anatoliy.  Jewish Asaf, Avshalom, Khaim, Malkiel, Meir, Mier, Moisey, Naum, Pinkhas, Ronit, Shaul.
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic) and Russian: patronymic from Abram , an East Slavic form of the personal name Abraham . It is formed with the Russian possessive suffix -ov.

variants.name: Abramowski
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hedwig, Horst.
Jewish (from Poland): patronymic from   Abram  , a shortened form of the personal name   Abraham  .

variants.name: Abrams
meanings: English: variant of Abram , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.
Jewish (Ashkenazic), Dutch, and German: patronymic from the personal name   Abram  .

variants.name: Abramovich
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Aron, Khaim, Lazer, Leib, Liba, Meir, Meyer, Moysey, Shaul, Shoshana, Sura.  Russian Lev, Igor, Boris, Mikhail, Anatoly, Raisa, Svetlana, Aleksandr, Anatoliy, Arkadiy, Garri, Grigoriy.
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): patronymic from   Abram  , a shortened form of the personal name   Abraham  .
Americanized form of Croatian Abramović, a cognate of 1 above.

variants.name: Abramowitz
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Hyman, Elkan, Mayer, Moshe, Shloma, Ari, Aron, Chain, Chana, Frima, Isador.
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): patronymic from Abram , a shortened form of the personal name Abraham . The ending -owitz is a Germanized form of the East Slavic patronymic ending -ovich, Polish -owicz.

variants.name: Abramson
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Hyman, Arie, Emanuel, Hanoch, Isadore, Alter, Fira, Hymie, Moshe, Myer, Rivka.
Jewish (Ashkenazic) and English: patronymic from Abram , a shortened form of the personal name Abraham , + son. Compare Abrahamson .

variants.name: Abramowicz
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Irena, Janusz, Ryszard, Waclaw, Wieslaw, Wlodzimierz, Yolanta, Zbigniew.  Jewish Chaim, Pola.
Polish and Jewish (from Poland): patronymic from   Abram  , a shortened form of the personal name   Abraham  .

variants.name: Abramczyk
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Alicja, Jaroslaw, Jerzy.  Jewish Mordechai, Shalom.
Polish and Jewish (from Poland): from a Polish pet form of the personal name   Abram   (see   Abraham  ).

variants.name: Abramyan
meanings: Armenian: patronymic from the personal name   Abram   or a Russianized form of   Abrahamyan  . This surname is found mainly in Russia. Compare   Abramian  .

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard Abram was born in 1497, in Kirkham, Lancashire, England, his father, Richardus Abraham, was 57 and his mother, Elizabeth Scott, was 55. He had at least 1 son with Margaret. He died in 1575, in Lancashire, England, at the age of 78.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard Abram est né en 1497, à Kirkham, Lancashire, Angleterre, son père, Richardus Abraham, avait 57 ans et sa mère, Elizabeth Scott, avait 55 ans. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Margaret. Il est décédé en 1575, à Lancashire, Angleterre, à l’âge de 78 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als Richard Abram 1497 in Kirkham, Lancashire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Richardus Abraham 57 und seine Mutter Elizabeth Scott 55. Er hatte mit Margaret mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1575 im Alter von 78 Jahren in Lancashire, England.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Abram è nato il nel 1497 nel Kirkham, Lancashire, England, il suo padre, Richardus Abraham, aveva 57 anni e il suo madre, Elizabeth Scott, aveva 55 anni. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Margaret. Morì nel 1575, nel Lancashire, England, all'età di 78 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: Richard Abram(이)가 1497년 영국 랭카실르 커카에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Richardus Abraham(은)는 57세였고, 그의 어머니 Elizabeth Scott(은)는 55세였다. 그는 Margaret(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1575년 영국 랭카실르에서 78세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Abram nasceu em 1497, em Kirkham, Lancashire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Richardus Abraham, tinha 57 anos e sua mãe, Elizabeth Scott, tinha 55 anos. Ele teve pelo menos 1 filho com Margaret. Ele faleceu em 1575, em Lancashire, Inglaterra, com 78 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard Abram родился в 1497 г. в местности кирхам, ланкашир, Англия, его отцу по имени Richardus Abraham было 57 лет, а матери, которую звали Elizabeth Scott, 55. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын от Margaret. Он умер в 1575 г. в местности ланкашир, Англия в возрасте 78 лет.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Richard Abram nació en 1497, en Kirkham, Lancashire, Inglaterra, su papá, Richardus Abraham, tenía 57 años y su mamá, Elizabeth Scott, tenía 55 años. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hijo con Margaret. Murió en 1575, en Lancashire, Inglaterra, a la edad de 78 años.
